title: EU Legislation Drafts : Prevention of Greenwashing and Right to Repair
bodyHtml:
According to a letter received from the Turkish Exporters Assembly, within the framework of the Circular Economy Action Plan, the European Commission aims to establish common rules at the EU level for sustainable and circular products under the Sustainable Product Initiative announced on March 30, 2023, with the aim of providing accurate information about product content to consumers and preventing greenwashing.

The letter further states that according to a study conducted by the European Commission in 2020, 53.3 percent of environmental claims on products in the EU market were found to be uncertain, unsupported, and misleading. It is stated that the absence of EU-level rules for environmental claims placed on products on a voluntary basis leads to greenwashing, misleading consumers and creating disadvantages for truly sustainable products.

In this context, the Green Claims Directive on Substantiation and Communication of Explicit Environmental Claims was published by the European Commission on March 22, 2023, with the aim of substantiating and communicating environmental claims. The draft imposes minimum requirements for substantiating and communicating voluntary claims in areas that are not regulated by EU legislation, excluding areas regulated by EU legislation such as eco-labels and organic food. The draft emphasizes the need for specific expressions of claims, such as "a T-shirt made from recycled plastic" or "packaging made from 30 percent recycled plastic."

Furthermore, considering the 230 different environmental labels currently available, the draft aims to prevent confusion for consumers by limiting the variety of labels, banning new labeling at the national level except for those established at the EU level, and allowing new special labels only if they pass the pre-approval process by proving that they meet the necessary requirements that are not fulfilled by existing labels.

In addition, the Common Rules Promoting the Repair of Goods Directive, announced by the Commission, makes it mandatory to repair products such as vacuum cleaners, tablets, and smartphones under legal guarantees as long as it is not more costly than renewing them. Rules have been proposed to enable consumers to access inexpensive and easy repair options after the warranty period expires.

Within this framework, recommendations have been made for facilitating manufacturers' access to relevant information when they want to repair their products, encouraging manufacturers to develop sustainable business models, informing consumers in advance about products that they need to repair themselves, establishing an online repair platform to match consumers and repairers, creating a European Repair Information Form for consumers to compare repair conditions and prices, and setting EU quality standards for repair services.

1- Proposal for a Directive on Green Claims 

2- Proposal for Empowering Consumers 

3- Proposal for Repair of Goods
